caveats: a: No published occupation mix specific to NAICS 921190 was located, so this is not an observed wage-weighted share.
a: Federal AI inventories do not represent state and local election boards, commissions, personnel offices, or supply agencies.
a: The estimate concerns government work even though the default commercial-firm lens appears nearly empty.
rho: Reported use cases are not equivalent to scaled production deployments or realized labor savings.
rho: Federal evidence may overstate capability and understate fragmentation among smaller state and local governments.
rho: Implementation in election, personnel, and civil-rights workflows can be delayed by due-process, records, cybersecurity, accessibility, and privacy requirements.
e: The frozen dataset provides no defensible LMM firm-count band, so the denominator for an empirical eligibility rate is absent.
e: Directory listings can contain self-assigned or stale NAICS codes and should not be treated as proof of industry eligibility.
e: This is a structural classification judgment, not an observed census of every entity using the code.
s5: Treating elections, appointments, agency reorganizations, or outsourcing awards as firm control transfers would violate the primitive definition.
s5: The frozen LMM firm-count input is missing.
q: Internal government budget retention or appropriations are not equivalent to commercial benefit retention.
q: Adjacent private government contractors belong to other NAICS industries and cannot be imported into this record merely to supply pricing evidence.
d5: Government employment and aggregate spending are broad inputs rather than direct measures of this six-digit service basket.
d5: Policy changes, fiscal stress, federal workforce reductions, election cycles, and intergovernmental shifts can move workloads sharply without changing underlying public need.
d5: Recent spending growth includes investment and functions outside general government support.
o: The boundary between operator-required quantity and automated workflow steps is not reported consistently by agencies.
o: The estimate applies to government operators performing the current service basket, not to a proven population of acquisitive commercial firms.
o: Future legislation could either mandate human review or authorize more end-to-end automated decisions.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The official industry is composed of government establishments that primarily support government and generally does not have a commercial external-customer revenue model. Any qualifying fringe case would need recurring arm's-length fees from external government customers, but no defensible population of such firms was identified.
lens.excluded_activities: Government establishments that do not sell recurring services to external customers; private HR, election-technology, consulting, procurement, staffing, and facilities-support vendors classified outside NAICS 921190; captive internal units; financing vehicles; and operations outside the roughly $1-10 million normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Any establishment correctly classified in NAICS 921190 that provides recurring general-government support, including government personnel services, election boards, civil-rights or civil-service commissions, general-services departments, and government supply agencies, and that also satisfies the default external-customer and LMM control-investment requirements.

a.bridge: GAO measured reported AI and generative-AI use cases at 11 selected federal agencies, not a wage-weighted task share in NAICS 921190. I map GAO's mission-support examples--written communications, information access, and program-status tracking--to the document, search, correspondence, reporting, HR, and procurement work implied by the Census activity list. This likely biases the estimate upward because the selected agencies were already reporting AI use cases and because election and civil-rights decisions carry unusually high accountability constraints.
caveats: No published occupation mix specific to NAICS 921190 was located, so this is not an observed wage-weighted share.
Federal AI inventories do not represent state and local election boards, commissions, personnel offices, or supply agencies.
The estimate concerns government work even though the default commercial-firm lens appears nearly empty.
a.change_evidence: A custom wage-weighted crosswalk of 2025 OEWS or government payroll occupations within NAICS 921190 to O*NET task statements, followed by agency-level time-and-motion studies separating already-automated from manual work, would materially change this range.
a.rationale: The industry's named activities combine document-heavy administrative work with managerial judgment, statutory interpretation, public accountability, and sensitive personnel or election data. AI can plausibly substitute for or avoid hiring in drafting, retrieval, classification, scheduling, routine case preparation, and reporting, but not the full accountable decision process. GAO found total reported AI use cases at selected agencies rose from 571 in 2023 to 1,110 in 2024 and generative-AI cases from 32 to 282, confirming material task applicability without measuring this primitive directly.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment for government excluding state and local education and hospitals, BEA measures broad real government consumption and investment, and Census measures total population; none isolates constant-quality demand for NAICS 921190's current service basket. I use near-flat projected government employment as the central quantity anchor, allow modest upside from population and administrative workload, and treat recent real-spending growth as a broad upper-side indicator. BEA capital and defense spending make the proxy more likely to overstate 921190 demand, while employment may understate service quantity if productivity rises.
caveats: Government employment and aggregate spending are broad inputs rather than direct measures of this six-digit service basket.
Policy changes, fiscal stress, federal workforce reductions, election cycles, and intergovernmental shifts can move workloads sharply without changing underlying public need.
Recent spending growth includes investment and functions outside general government support.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain five-year workload forecasts and historical unit volumes from a stratified sample of government HR offices, election boards, civil-rights commissions, general-services departments, and supply agencies, normalized for case complexity and service quality rather than headcount or nominal budgets.
d5.rationale: The functions named by Census--personnel administration, elections, civil-service and civil-rights commissions, general services, and supply administration--remain tied to recurring statutory and operating needs. BLS projects government employment excluding state and local education and hospitals to decline only 0.1% from 2024 to 2034. Recent evidence is more variable: BEA reports real government consumption and investment changes of -1.2% in 2022, 3.5% in 2023, and 3.8% in 2024, while Census reports U.S. population grew 0.5% from July 2024 to July 2025. Together these support approximately flat to modestly higher five-year service quantity, with budget contraction as the downside.

caveats: The frozen dataset provides no defensible LMM firm-count band, so the denominator for an empirical eligibility rate is absent.
Directory listings can contain self-assigned or stale NAICS codes and should not be treated as proof of industry eligibility.
This is a structural classification judgment, not an observed census of every entity using the code.
e.change_evidence: A record-level business registry or commercial establishment database filtered to NAICS 921190, followed by manual verification of ownership, external recurring revenue, normalized EBITDA, and correct NAICS classification, could establish whether any eligible fringe population exists.
e.rationale: Census defines 921190 as government establishments primarily providing general support for government. Such establishments are not firms with normalized EBITDA and transferable equity, while private outsourced vendors performing adjacent HR, consulting, technology, procurement, or facilities work are ordinarily classified by their private-sector activity. The base therefore treats the correctly classified LMM firm population as empty; the high allows only for rare coding anomalies or quasi-government entities with a genuinely transferable external-service operation.

l.detail: No receipts concept for this code (government / SUSB out of scope). QCEW government wages exist ($34,560,807,287) but there is no revenue denominator; labor_share left null.
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0000 (margins.json[92]: ESTIMATE: public administration — EBITDA is not meaningful for government units; margin 0 makes the n_band chain yield 0 by construction (recorded as a known gap)); revenue/employee $374,583 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at --); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: The boundary between operator-required quantity and automated workflow steps is not reported consistently by agencies.
The estimate applies to government operators performing the current service basket, not to a proven population of acquisitive commercial firms.
Future legislation could either mandate human review or authorize more end-to-end automated decisions.
o.change_evidence: Review statutes, delegations of authority, administrative-procedure requirements, service-channel telemetry, and production automation results across representative federal, state, and local 921190 establishments to measure which completed transactions no longer require accountable human or organizational sign-off.
o.rationale: Most year-five quantity should still require an accountable government operator because the named activities include election administration, civil-rights and civil-service commissions, personnel authority, public procurement, and general-services decisions. AI and self-service can remove intake, search, drafting, routing, and status-check work, but software alone cannot hold statutory authority, certify elections, make appealable personnel decisions, or bear public accountability. The range allows substantial elimination of routine transaction quantity without assuming that implementation difficulty belongs here.

caveats: Internal government budget retention or appropriations are not equivalent to commercial benefit retention.
Adjacent private government contractors belong to other NAICS industries and cannot be imported into this record merely to supply pricing evidence.
q.change_evidence: For any verified eligible fringe firms, collect customer-level contracts, rate cards, renewal histories, bid tabs, gross-margin bridges, and cohort retention data to distinguish fixed-fee, cost-reimbursement, and labor-hour economics and quantify repricing after automation.
q.rationale: The official industry consists of government establishments producing public-administration support rather than commercial firms billing external customers. Without a verified eligible-firm population, contracts, renewals, or market pricing, there is no defensible basis for estimating the discounted share of implemented gross benefit retained by an operator.

rho.bridge: GAO's agency use-case counts and reported obstacles measure adoption activity across selected federal agencies, not the five-year implemented share of exposed opportunity in NAICS 921190. I translate rapid use-case growth into a meaningful implementation path but discount for privacy, policy, budget, technical-resource, and change constraints. Selection of AI-active agencies likely biases implementation upward relative to the full federal, state, and local population.
caveats: Reported use cases are not equivalent to scaled production deployments or realized labor savings.
Federal evidence may overstate capability and understate fragmentation among smaller state and local governments.
Implementation in election, personnel, and civil-rights workflows can be delayed by due-process, records, cybersecurity, accessibility, and privacy requirements.
rho.change_evidence: Obtain deployment-level inventories from federal, state, county, and municipal general-services, personnel, and election offices showing production status, user penetration, realized hours saved, exception rates, audit findings, and procurement lead times through 2031.
rho.rationale: Implementation should lag technical exposure. GAO reported a ninefold rise in generative-AI use cases from 2023 to 2024, but officials at 10 of 12 selected agencies said existing federal policy, including privacy policy, could obstruct adoption; they also cited technical resources, budget, and rapidly changing policies. A 2026 GAO review found OPM proposing AI and IT modernization after a 35% staff decline, showing pressure to implement while also highlighting institutional-knowledge and operating-capacity risks.

caveats: Treating elections, appointments, agency reorganizations, or outsourcing awards as firm control transfers would violate the primitive definition.
The frozen LMM firm-count input is missing.
s5.change_evidence: First identify verified private, externally serving, correctly classified LMM firms; then obtain five years of control-transfer records from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, and state transaction filings for that verified cohort, including no-sale observations.
s5.rationale: No defensible probability can be defined because no eligible private-firm population was established. Government departments, boards, and commissions can be reorganized or have leadership turnover, but those events are not qualifying control transfers of transferable operating firms.

principal_driver: Document-heavy recurring government administration gives AI a credible role in avoiding hiring and improving throughput while statutory demand persists.
principal_weakness: NAICS 921190 is structurally a government-establishment category, leaving no verified transferable LMM firm population or commercial retention model.

caveats: S1 is an archived 1997 NAICS page rather than an explicitly current-edition definition. It supports the exact classification text used, but does not itself establish that the wording remained current as of the review date.

S2 measures selected federal agencies' reported use-case inventories, not wage-weighted task exposure, scaled production implementation, realized labor savings, or state and local behavior. The record labels and bridges this limitation honestly, so it remains a caveat rather than an undisclosed proxy.

The d5 range combines broad government employment, aggregate real government spending, and total-population indicators that do not directly measure constant-quality service quantity in NAICS 921190. The bridge discloses this mismatch, but the relatively tight 0.96-1.08 range remains weakly anchored.

S6 describes one federal agency's workforce reduction and proposed technology response, not realized AI implementation. It provides only directional support for rho, as the record substantially acknowledges.

sources_audited.note: The Census page defines 921190 as government establishments providing general government support and lists the cited personnel, election-board, commission, general-services, personnel-office, and supply-agency examples.

GAO reports 571 to 1,110 total AI use cases and 32 to 282 generative-AI use cases across the stated selected-agency populations, names the cited mission-support uses, and documents the cited policy, privacy, technical-resource, budget, and policy-maintenance obstacles.

The BLS release's industry-sector chart reports a projected 0.1 percent decline for government, with the stated exclusion of state and local education and hospitals and wage-and-salary-employment scope.

BEA Table A11 reports real government consumption expenditures and gross investment changes of -1.2 percent, 3.5 percent, and 3.8 percent for 2022-2024, and its note says government consumption services are valued at production cost.
The Census release reports U.S. population growth of 1.8 million, or 0.5 percent, from July 1, 2024 to July 1, 2025.

GAO reports OPM headcount fell 35 percent between December 2024 and March 2026, 57 percent of separating employees had at least 11 years of service, and OPM proposed AI tools and IT modernization in offices with fewer staff.

sources.title: Artificial Intelligence: Generative AI Use and Management at Federal Agencies

title: Other General Government Support
